questions.prompt: What is the root cause of most flaky Selenium failures?
options: The browser driver is the wrong version
The test tries to act on an element before the page has rendered it
The assertions are written incorrectly
The test machine is out of memory
questions.explanation: Tests run faster than asynchronous pages load. Acting on an element before it exists causes intermittent pass/fail — a synchronization problem.

questions.prompt: How does an implicit wait differ from an explicit wait?
options: Implicit waits target one condition; explicit waits apply globally
Implicit waits apply globally to every lookup; explicit waits target one step and condition
They are two names for the same mechanism
Implicit waits only work in headless mode
questions.explanation: An implicit wait is a single global retry setting for all findElement calls. An explicit WebDriverWait targets a specific step and waits for a specific condition.

questions.prompt: Which ExpectedConditions check fits 'wait until the element can be safely clicked'?
options: ExpectedConditions.titleIs
ExpectedConditions.elementToBeClickable
ExpectedConditions.alertIsPresent
ExpectedConditions.numberOfWindowsToBe
questions.explanation: elementToBeClickable waits until the element is both visible and enabled, which is exactly the condition you need before calling click().

questions.prompt: Why is Thread.sleep(5000) a bad way to fix a flaky step?
options: It is not valid Java
It always waits the full time even when ready, yet still fails if the page is slower
It only works on Chrome
It clears the browser cache as a side effect
questions.explanation: A fixed sleep waits for the clock, not the condition: it wastes time when the element is ready instantly and still fails when the page takes longer than the guess.

options: After the full timeout has elapsed, always
The moment the element becomes visible, or it throws TimeoutException at the deadline
Immediately, without checking anything
Only after the page is reloaded
questions.explanation: An explicit wait returns as soon as its condition is met, so fast pages are not penalised; if the condition is never met it throws TimeoutException when the timeout passes.
